manifest.erp_milestone: Bootstrap the MiniERP project: a single runnable entry point (main.py) guarded by `if __name__ == "__main__":` that prints a welcome banner and the running Python version (read live from sys.version_info), plus a tiny reusable `banner()`/`welcome()` helper, all written under PEP 8 with a module docstring. By the end of the phase the learner can run MiniERP both as a script (`python main.py`) and as a module (`python -m main`) and see a clean, version-aware startup screen — the seed of the shared core that every later interface (CLI, Web, GUI, TUI) will boot from.

lessons.title: How Python Runs Your Code
lessons.summary: A gentle but precise tour of what actually happens when you run Python: installing 3.14, the interpreter and REPL, the source -> bytecode -> virtual-machine pipeline (with a real `dis` peek), the difference between running a script and importing a module, the `__name__ == "__main__"` guard, and isolating dependencies with venv and pip. The learner lays the very first stone of MiniERP: a guarded entry point that prints a welcome banner and the live Python version. Stdlib-only (sys, dis, platform); pip/venv are introduced conceptually and used to create the project's own environment.

tasks.title: Install Python 3.14 and meet the REPL
python_features: implicit echo of expression results in the REPL
_ as the last-result variable in the REPL
learning_goals: Install CPython 3.14 on Windows/macOS/Linux and confirm it from a terminal with `python --version`
Understand that 'Python' the language is run by an interpreter (CPython) — a program that executes your program
Start, use, and leave the interactive REPL, and know the difference between the REPL and running a file
Recognize that in the REPL a bare expression is auto-printed and `_` holds the previous result, but a script file does not auto-print
tasks.erp_increment: No code change yet — orient the learner. Frame MiniERP as the suite they will build, and have them confirm their toolchain by launching the REPL and typing `import sys; sys.version` to read the interpreter that will run every future task.
tasks.starter_stub: Read-only concept page. No task.py edits required. Includes a checklist: open a terminal, run `python --version` (expect 3.14.x), launch the REPL, evaluate `2 + 2` and observe the echoed `4`, then evaluate `_` to see it again, then exit with Ctrl-D / exit().
tasks.test_focus: None (theory page, no checking).

tasks.title: Source, bytecode, and the virtual machine
python_features: compilation pipeline: source text -> AST -> bytecode -> CPython VM
bytecode is an implementation detail, not the language spec
learning_goals: Explain, at a high level, that Python is compiled to bytecode and then executed by a virtual machine — it is not 'interpreted line by line' the way many assume, and it is not compiled to native machine code like C
Know where the compiled cache lives (__pycache__/*.pyc) and why deleting it is harmless
Distinguish the language (the spec) from CPython (one implementation) and other implementations (PyPy, etc.)
tasks.erp_increment: Still orientation: explain that every MiniERP module the learner writes will travel this same source -> bytecode -> VM path, so 'reading the bytecode' (next task) is a real tool for understanding their own ERP code.
tasks.starter_stub: Read-only concept page with a diagram-in-text of the pipeline and a pointer forward to the hands-on `dis` task. No task.py edits.
tasks.test_focus: None (theory page, no checking).

tasks.title: Peek under the hood with dis
python_features: functions as first-class objects you can disassemble
learning_goals: Use `dis.dis(obj)` to disassemble a function and read the resulting opcodes
Connect a one-line arithmetic function to the LOAD_CONST / BINARY_OP / RETURN_VALUE instructions the VM will execute
Build the mental model that a function carries a code object (`__code__`) which is what actually gets run
tasks.erp_increment: Introduce the future ERP money math gently: write a trivial `line_total(qty, price)` that returns `qty * price` and disassemble it. This is the conceptual seed of invoice line totals built much later — here we only look at how the multiply becomes bytecode.
tasks.starter_stub: task.py defines `def line_total(qty, price): return qty * price` and a `if __name__ == "__main__":` block that calls `import dis; dis.dis(line_total)`. Learner runs it; expected stdout is the disassembly (an 'output' task compares the run).
tasks.test_focus: Output task: compare program stdout to the expected disassembly of the multiply, teaching the learner to actually run code and read the VM's view of it.

tasks.title: Running a script vs. importing a module
python_features: import side effects (top-level code runs on import)
the difference between executing and importing
module-level code runs exactly once per process
the import search path (sys.path) at a high level
learning_goals: Predict what runs when you execute a file directly versus when you import it
Understand that importing a module executes its top-level statements (so top-level side effects fire on import)
Know the two ways to launch code — as a path (`python main.py`) and as a module (`python -m main`) — which motivates the next task's guard
tasks.erp_increment: Set up the rule MiniERP will follow forever: domain/service code lives in importable modules with NO side effects at import time; only the entry point runs things. This page states that contract before the learner writes the entry point.
tasks.starter_stub: Read-only concept page. Demonstrates with a tiny two-file example (a module that prints at import time vs. one that defines a function) and explains why import-time printing is a bug for a library. No task.py edits.
tasks.test_focus: None (theory page, no checking).

tasks.title: The __main__ guard and MiniERP's first entry point
learning_goals: Write the canonical `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard and explain that __name__ is "__main__" only when the file is run directly, and the module's own name when imported
Separate reusable logic (a function) from the run-only entry block
Create the project's first real, importable-and-runnable module with a docstring
tasks.erp_increment: FIRST real MiniERP code. Create the entry point with a `welcome()` function that returns the welcome string 'Welcome to MiniERP' and a guard that prints it. Importing the module must print nothing; running it must print the welcome line. This is the literal bootstrap of the capstone.
tasks.starter_stub:
task.py:
"""MiniERP entry point."""


def welcome() -> str:
    """Return the MiniERP welcome line."""
    # TODO: return the exact string 'Welcome to MiniERP'
    ...


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # TODO: print the result of welcome()
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: import the module (assert nothing printed at import, i.e. importing has no side effects) and assert `welcome()` returns exactly 'Welcome to MiniERP'. This is the first piece of code that propagates forward in the framework lesson.

tasks.title: Report the running Python version
learning_goals: Import and use `sys` to read the live interpreter version rather than hard-coding it
Access named fields of sys.version_info (major, minor, micro) and format them
Appreciate that the program can introspect the very interpreter running it (ties back to lesson 1's 'CPython runs your code')
tasks.erp_increment: Extend the entry point: add `python_line() -> str` returning e.g. 'Running on Python 3.14.3' built from sys.version_info, and have the guard print both the welcome and the version line. Delivers the second half of the phase milestone (banner + live version).
tasks.starter_stub:
import sys


def welcome() -> str:
    """Return the MiniERP welcome line."""
    return "Welcome to MiniERP"


def python_line() -> str:
    """Return 'Running on Python <major>.<minor>.<micro>'."""
    v = sys.version_info
    # TODO: build the string from v.major, v.minor, v.micro
    ...


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(welcome())
    # TODO: also print python_line()
tasks.test_focus: unittest: assert `python_line()` starts with 'Running on Python ' and that it contains the *actual* `f"{sys.version_info.major}.{sys.version_info.minor}.{sys.version_info.micro}"` (computed in the test, not hard-coded), proving the value is read live.

tasks.title: Isolate dependencies with venv and pip
python_features: why global installs are a trap
learning_goals: Create and activate a virtual environment for the MiniERP project and explain why isolation matters
Use `python -m pip` to install/list packages and understand where they land (site-packages)
Distinguish stdlib (always available) from third-party packages (must be installed) — reinforcing this course's stdlib-first policy
tasks.erp_increment: Operational milestone: the learner creates the project's `.venv`. No third-party package is required yet (everything so far is stdlib), establishing the clean, isolated environment that all later phases (pytest, FastAPI, Textual, etc.) will install into.
tasks.starter_stub: Read-only concept page with copy-paste commands for Windows/macOS/Linux: `python -m venv .venv`, activate, `python -m pip install --upgrade pip`, `python -m pip list`. Notes that MiniERP needs no dependencies yet — that's a feature. No task.py edits.
tasks.test_focus: None (theory page, no checking).

lessons.title: Names, Objects, and Identity
lessons.summary: The conceptual heart of Python for newcomers: dynamic typing, the object model where literally everything is an object, the crucial difference between identity (`is`, `id()`) and equality (`==`), the singleton `None`, truthiness and `bool()`, and basic I/O with `print()` and `input()`. The lesson closes with the cultural foundations — comments, docstrings, PEP 8, and the Zen of Python — and uses them to clean up and harden the MiniERP entry point built in lesson 1.

tasks.title: Everything is an object
python_features: the object model (everything is an object)
objects have a type, an identity, and a value
functions, modules, classes, and even types are objects
attributes and the dunder world (preview of __class__, __doc__)
learning_goals: State and internalize that in Python every value — ints, strings, functions, modules, even classes and types themselves — is an object with a type
Use `type(x)` to ask any value what it is
See that the `welcome` function and the module from lesson 1 are themselves objects with attributes (e.g. `welcome.__doc__`)
tasks.erp_increment: Reflective, not additive: use the existing MiniERP entry point as the specimen. Inspect `type(welcome)`, `welcome.__doc__`, and `type(welcome())` to show the learner's own ERP code is made of objects.
tasks.starter_stub: Read-only concept page that imports nothing new; it references the lesson-1 entry point and shows REPL transcripts of `type(welcome)`, `welcome.__name__`, `welcome.__doc__`. No task.py edits.
tasks.test_focus: None (theory page, no checking).

tasks.title: Variables are names, not boxes (dynamic typing)
python_features: variables as names bound to objects (name binding vs. 'storage')
rebinding a name to a different type
type() to observe a name's current object
the distinction between a name and the object it references
learning_goals: Explain that a Python variable is a name bound to an object, and the same name can be rebound to objects of different types over time
Predict the result of rebinding a name and checking its type
Reject the 'variable is a box that holds a value' mental model in favor of the 'name points at an object' model
tasks.erp_increment: Add a tiny configuration probe used later by the banner: a function `describe(value) -> str` that returns `f'{value!r} is a {type(value).__name__}'`. This becomes a debugging helper the ERP uses to report config values; here it demonstrates that one name can describe ints, strings, None, etc.
tasks.starter_stub:
def describe(value) -> str:
    """Return e.g. \"42 is a int\" describing value's object type."""
    # TODO: use repr via !r and type(value).__name__
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: assert `describe(42) == "42 is a int"`, `describe('x') == "'x' is a str"`, and `describe(None) == 'None is a NoneType'`, exercising dynamic typing across multiple object types through one name.

tasks.title: Identity vs. equality: is, ==, and id()
python_features: small-integer and string interning caveats (explained, not relied upon)
why `is` is correct for None/True/False and `==` for values
object identity as the address-like number from id()
learning_goals: Distinguish identity (same object in memory, `is` / matching `id()`) from equality (same value, `==`)
Know the rule of thumb: compare to None/True/False with `is`, compare values with `==`, and never use `is` to compare numbers or strings for value
Understand interning as an implementation detail that must not be depended on
tasks.erp_increment: Add `same_object(a, b) -> bool` returning `a is b`, the identity check the ERP audit log will later use to detect whether two records are literally the same object versus merely equal. Pure-stdlib, no new imports.
tasks.starter_stub:
def same_object(a, b) -> bool:
    """Return True only if a and b are the SAME object (identity)."""
    # TODO: use the identity operator, not ==
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: with `x = ['p']; y = ['p']`, assert `same_object(x, x) is True` (identity) but `same_object(x, y) is False` even though `x == y` (equality), and assert `same_object(None, None) is True`. Reinforces is-vs-== without relying on interning.

tasks.title: None and truthiness
python_features: truthy vs falsy values (0, 0.0, '', [], {}, None are falsy)
learning_goals: Identify which built-in values are falsy and which are truthy, and predict `bool(x)` for each
Use `is None` rather than `== None` to test for the absence of a value
Recognize that a function with no return statement returns None implicitly
tasks.erp_increment: Add `display_name(name) -> str` that returns the given customer name, but falls back to 'Guest' when the name is None or empty/whitespace — the first taste of MiniERP input-hardening (real customers later get this same defaulting). Uses truthiness and `is None`.
tasks.starter_stub:
def display_name(name) -> str:
    """Return name if it is a non-empty string, else 'Guest'.

    Treat None and blank/whitespace-only strings as missing.
    """
    # TODO: handle None with `is None`, then test truthiness of name.strip()
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: assert `display_name('Ada') == 'Ada'`, `display_name(None) == 'Guest'`, `display_name('') == 'Guest'`, and `display_name('   ') == 'Guest'`, exercising None-handling and truthiness/falsiness.

tasks.title: Talking to the user: print and input
python_features: print() with sep and end parameters
input() and that it always returns str
writing to sys.stdout vs returning values (separation of compute and I/O)
learning_goals: Use print()'s `sep` and `end` parameters deliberately instead of building strings by hand
Know that input() returns a string (never an int) and that trimming/parsing is the caller's job
Keep I/O at the edges: have logic-returning functions stay pure and let the entry point do the printing
tasks.erp_increment: Add `banner(title, width=40) -> str` that returns a centered title between two rule lines (e.g. '=' * width), and wire the entry point's guard to `print(banner('MiniERP'))` followed by the version line. This is the actual welcome banner promised by the phase milestone, kept as a pure function so it stays testable.
tasks.starter_stub:
def banner(title: str, width: int = 40) -> str:
    """Return a 3-line banner: a rule, the centered title, a rule.

    Lines are joined by '\n'. Use '=' * width for the rules
    and str.center for the title line.
    """
    # TODO: build and return the three-line string (no printing here)
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: assert `banner('MiniERP', 10)` equals the exact expected 3-line string ('=========='+chr(10)+' MiniERP '.center... actually 'MiniERP'.center(10) + chr(10) + '=========='), proving the function is pure (returns, not prints) and that centering/width work.

tasks.title: Comments, docstrings, and self-documenting code
python_features: the difference between a comment (for readers of source) and a docstring (queryable at runtime via __doc__)
learning_goals: Write a proper one-line and multi-line docstring per PEP 257 and explain how it differs from a `#` comment
Retrieve a docstring at runtime via `__doc__` (and know `help()` reads it)
Add a meaningful module docstring to the MiniERP entry point
tasks.erp_increment: Document the entry point: ensure the module has a top docstring and that `banner`/`welcome`/`python_line` each carry an accurate one-line docstring. The ERP now describes itself — future `help(module)` and generated docs (Sphinx phase) depend on these.
tasks.starter_stub:
"""MiniERP — a tiny small-business ERP suite (entry point).

TODO: write a 2-3 line module docstring summarizing what MiniERP is.
"""


def welcome() -> str:
    # TODO: add a one-line docstring, then return 'Welcome to MiniERP'
    ...
tasks.test_focus: unittest: assert the module's `__doc__` is a non-empty string mentioning 'MiniERP', and that `welcome.__doc__`/`banner.__doc__`/`python_line.__doc__` are all non-empty, enforcing the docstring habit.

tasks.title: PEP 8 and the Zen of Python
python_features: PEP 8 (naming, indentation 4 spaces, line length, snake_case vs CapWords, blank-line conventions)
readability counts / explicit is better than implicit
the cultural norms that make idiomatic Python
preview of automated style tools (ruff/black) as later-phase reinforcement
learning_goals: Recall the core PEP 8 rules (4-space indent, snake_case for functions/variables, CapWords for classes, two blank lines between top-level defs)
Recognize the Zen of Python aphorisms and what `import this` does
Build the instinct that style is communication, setting up the later ruff/mypy tooling phase
tasks.erp_increment: Codify the house style for all future MiniERP code: this knowledge check pins down the naming and layout conventions every later module must follow, so contributions stay consistent across the four interfaces.
tasks.starter_stub: Multiple-choice page. Example item: 'Which name is PEP 8 compliant for a function that totals an invoice line?' with options like `LineTotal`, `lineTotal`, `line_total`, `line-total` (correct: `line_total`). Another item asks what `import this` prints (the Zen of Python). No task.py code to complete.
tasks.test_focus: choice task: correct answers are the PEP 8-compliant identifiers and the Zen-of-Python recognition; distractors are camelCase/CapWords/hyphenated names and wrong descriptions of `import this`.

manifest.erp_milestone: MiniERP gains a correct money model and a text/identifier toolkit. By the end of this phase the codebase has a `money` module that represents prices and totals with `decimal.Decimal` (never float), rounds money to cents with a documented rounding mode, formats currency strings, and computes line totals, tax, and change with `divmod`/`Fraction`; plus a `text` module that normalizes and validates customer/product text input (strip, casefold, Unicode normalization), generates and parses canonical SKU identifiers via slicing and the format-spec mini-language, and encodes/decodes records to/from bytes for storage and export. These two pure modules become the shared numeric+text foundation every later interface (CLI, Web, GUI, TUI) reuses.

lessons.title: Numbers and Money
lessons.summary: Build MiniERP's numeric core. Learners meet Python's numeric tower (int with arbitrary precision, float and its traps, bool, complex), the full arithmetic and bitwise toolkit (//, /, %, divmod, pow, augmented assignment, &|^~<<>>), and chained comparisons, then discover why binary float is wrong for money and rebuild prices on decimal.Decimal with an explicit context and rounding mode, using Fraction for exact ratios. Output: a `money` module that stores cents-accurate money, splits and rounds totals, and applies tax and discount ratios correctly.

tasks.title: The numeric tower: int, float, bool, complex
learning_goals: Understand that Python int has unbounded precision while float is a fixed-width binary double
Know that bool is a subclass of int (True == 1) and where that helps and hurts
Recognize complex as a first-class numeric type and when it is irrelevant to business code
Read integer literals in hex, octal, binary, and with digit-group underscores
tasks.erp_increment: Read-only concept page framed around MiniERP: explains why we will choose specific numeric types for quantities (int), money (Decimal, coming soon), and never complex. No code changes to the shared module yet; sets up the design decisions for the rest of the phase.
tasks.starter_stub:
# Concept page only. Sample snippets the learner can run in the console:
#   big = 2 ** 200            # int never overflows
#   print(big.bit_length())
#   print(True + True)        # 2  -> bool is an int
#   print(0xFF, 0o17, 0b1010, 1_000_000)
#   z = 3 + 4j; print(z.real, z.imag, abs(z))
# No task.py edits required for this theory task.
tasks.test_focus: No automated checks (theory page). Comprehension is reinforced by the following choice task.

tasks.title: Why 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3
learning_goals: Internalize that binary float cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly
Know that == on floats is unreliable and money must never be a float
Recognize math.isclose as the tolerant comparison and why it is still wrong for currency
tasks.erp_increment: Knowledge check that justifies the phase's central ERP decision (money uses Decimal). No code change; the correct mental model gates the Decimal refactor in later tasks.
tasks.starter_stub:
# Multiple-choice task. Learner evaluates statements such as:
#   - What does (0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3) evaluate to?
#   - Which is the right way to store a $19.99 price for an invoice?
#   - Is math.isclose() acceptable for deciding if an invoice is fully paid?
# Answers are encoded in the EduTools task config, not in task.py.
tasks.test_focus: Choice task: a single correct option (float == is False; money must use Decimal; isclose is unsuitable for exact currency).

tasks.title: Arithmetic, division, and divmod
learning_goals: Distinguish / (always float) from // (floor) and use each deliberately
Use divmod to get quotient and remainder in one call for packing/change-making
Use 3-argument pow for modular arithmetic
Predict results of mixed int/float arithmetic

These pure functions start the shared inventory/packing math layer.

tasks.title: Augmented assignment and chained comparisons
learning_goals: Use augmented assignment for running totals and counters
Write range checks as chained comparisons instead of two and-ed comparisons
Understand chained comparison short-circuit semantics (a < b < c evaluates b once)

tasks.title: Bitwise operators and bit flags
learning_goals: Combine, test, and clear boolean attributes packed into a single integer using masks
Read and write shifts and understand << as multiply-by-power-of-two
Use int.bit_count() to count set bits
tasks.erp_increment: Add a compact product-attribute flag system to `task.py` (a precursor to the later Users & Roles permission bitset): constants like TAXABLE, DISCOUNTABLE, RETURNABLE as 1<<n, plus `has_flag(flags, mask)`, `set_flag`, and `clear_flag`. Lets MiniERP store several product booleans in one int field.

tasks.title: Modeling money with Decimal
python_features: Decimal from str vs float (why str)
learning_goals: Construct Decimal from strings to preserve exact decimal values
Set and read the decimal context and choose an explicit rounding mode for money
Quantize amounts to two decimal places (cents) deterministically
Explain why Decimal('0.1') + Decimal('0.2') == Decimal('0.3')
tasks.erp_increment: Establish MiniERP's canonical money representation in `task.py`: `money(value)` builds a Decimal from a string/int safely, and `round_money(amount)` quantizes to cents with a documented rounding mode. This is the money foundation every monetary value in the ERP will pass through from now on.
tasks.starter_stub:

    """Build an exact money Decimal. Reject float input."""
    # TODO: Decimal(str(value)); raise on float
    raise NotImplementedError

def round_money(amount: Decimal) -> Decimal:
    """Quantize to cents using ROUND_HALF_UP."""
    # TODO: amount.quantize(CENTS, rounding=ROUND_HALF_UP)
    raise NotImplementedError
tasks.test_focus: Tests assert money('0.1')+money('0.2')==money('0.3'), that float input is rejected, and that round_money applies ROUND_HALF_UP correctly (e.g. 2.005 -> 2.01) and produces exactly two decimal places.

tasks.title: Tax, discounts, and exact ratios with Fraction
learning_goals: Represent a tax or discount rate as an exact ratio rather than a binary float
Apply a rate to a money amount and round the result to cents once, at the end
Understand when Fraction (exact ratio) vs Decimal (fixed decimal) is the right tool

    """Multiply money by a rate and round to cents."""
    # TODO: round_money(amount * rate)
    raise NotImplementedError

def tax_for(amount: Decimal, percent: Fraction) -> Decimal:
    """Tax on `amount` given `percent` as a Fraction of 100."""

tasks.test_focus: Tests check exact tax computations (e.g. 8.25% of 100.00 -> 8.25), single final rounding (no double-rounding drift), and that a discount + tax pipeline totals to the expected cent value.

lessons.title: Text, Identifiers and Bytes
lessons.summary: Build MiniERP's text and serialization core on top of the money module. Learners master str (indexing, all slicing forms, the full method surface), every string-formatting system Python offers (f-strings including self-documenting '=', the format-spec mini-language, str.format, %-formatting, and string.Template), raw strings, escapes and Unicode handling, and finally the bytes/bytearray/memoryview layer with encode/decode and codecs. Output: a `text` module that cleans and normalizes input, builds and parses canonical SKUs, formats currency and invoice lines, and serializes records to bytes for storage/export.

tasks.title: String indexing and slicing
learning_goals: Index strings with positive and negative positions
Slice with start/stop/step including reversal and skipping
Rely on slice bound-clamping instead of manual length checks

tasks.title: Warehouse Bin Coordinates with Complex Numbers
python_features: complex() constructor with real and imaginary args
abs() of a complex as Euclidean magnitude / vector length
complex arithmetic: + and - for vector displacement between points
scalar multiply/divide of a complex (a + b) / 2 for a midpoint
complex equality and the fact .real/.imag are always float
mixing complex with int/float in the numeric tower (auto-coercion)
round() applied to the float result of abs()
min() with a key= function over complex points
f-string formatting of a float distance, e.g. f'{d:.2f}'
learning_goals: Use a Python complex value as a lightweight, immutable 2D point (x = aisle along .real, y = shelf along .imag) instead of inventing a custom pair type.
Read the coordinates back out with the .real and .imag attributes, remembering they are always float even when you passed ints.
Compute the straight-line travel distance between two bins with abs(b - a): subtraction gives the displacement vector, abs() gives its length (the Pythagorean magnitude).
Combine points arithmetically: add/subtract to translate, and divide by a scalar to find a midpoint or average position.
Understand .conjugate() (mirror across the aisle axis) and why abs(z) and abs(z.conjugate()) are equal.
Pick the nearest bin to the packing dock using min(..., key=lambda bin: abs(bin.coord - dock)), turning a rare numeric type into a real pick-path optimization.
See how complex participates in the numeric tower: it coerces ints/floats automatically, but unlike int/float it has no ordering, so you must compare via abs().
tasks.erp_increment: Adds a geometry helper to the Inventory module so warehouse bins carry a physical location. New module mini_erp/inventory/geo.py defines warehouse bin coordinates as complex numbers (real part = aisle, imag part = shelf/position) and pure functions over them: make_location(aisle, position) -> complex (wraps complex()), coordinates(loc) -> tuple[float, float] (returns (loc.real, loc.imag)), travel_distance(a, b) -> float (returns abs(b - a), the pick path length between two bins), midpoint(a, b) -> complex ((a + b) / 2, e.g. for a staging point), and nearest_bin(dock, bins) which selects the closest bin to the packing dock using min(bins, key=lambda b: travel_distance(dock, b)). This gives the Products & Inventory module its first spatial query — 'which bin is closest to ship from' — and is consumed later by the Reporting module's pick-efficiency stats. It depends only on numeric features from earlier p02 tasks (the numeric-tower task already introduced complex literals and the complex() constructor) and built-in min()/round().
tasks.starter_stub: Create mini_erp/inventory/geo.py. Implement make_location(aisle, position) returning complex(aisle, position); coordinates(loc) returning (loc.real, loc.imag); travel_distance(a, b) returning abs(b - a); midpoint(a, b) returning (a + b) / 2; and nearest_bin(dock, bins) returning the bin from the non-empty iterable bins with the smallest travel_distance(dock, bin), using min(..., key=...). Add module and function docstrings explaining that a bin location is modeled as a complex point where .real is the aisle and .imag is the shelf position, and that abs() of a displacement is the Pythagorean travel distance. Keep every function pure (no printing, no mutation).
tasks.test_focus: Unit-test each function against hand-computed values: make_location(3, 4) == (3+4j) and its .real/.imag are 3.0/4.0 (assert they are float, not int); coordinates((3+4j)) == (3.0, 4.0); travel_distance((1+2j), (4+6j)) == 5.0 (the classic 3-4-5 triangle) and travel_distance is symmetric (a->b equals b->a) via abs() of the conjugate-related displacement; midpoint((1+2j),(3+6j)) == (2+4j); a .conjugate() check asserting abs(z) == abs(z.conjugate()); and nearest_bin returns the correct closest bin given a dock at the origin and several candidate bins (including a tie-break sanity case and a single-element iterable). Also assert that complex coordinates carry float parts even when built from ints, and that round(travel_distance(...), 2) formats as expected for an irrational distance like abs(1+1j).

tasks.title: Decimal context traps and signal flags: enforcing penny-exactness
python_features: decimal.localcontext as a parenthesized context manager
decimal context traps mapping: ctx.traps[Inexact] = True
decimal signals/exceptions: Inexact, InvalidOperation, DivisionByZero, Rounded
intentionally raising InvalidOperation (and catching it to re-raise ValueError)
reading and clearing accumulated condition flags: getcontext().flags, ctx.flags[Inexact], ctx.clear_flags()
the difference between a trapped signal (raises) and an untrapped signal (sets a flag, returns a rounded result)
Decimal.quantize with an explicit rounding mode (ROUND_DOWN) for floor-to-cents
try/except inside a context manager; control flow that falls back on a caught signal
PEP 604 return annotations (list[Decimal]), walrus operator, f-string '=' self-documenting debug output
learning_goals: Explain the difference between a Decimal *signal* (a condition like Inexact or InvalidOperation), a *trap* (signals you have asked the context to raise as an exception), and a *flag* (a sticky record that a condition occurred even when not trapped).
Turn a context trap on for a bounded region with localcontext() and ctx.traps[Inexact] = True, so that an operation that cannot be represented exactly raises instead of silently rounding.
Use a trapped Inexact as control flow: attempt an exact equal split, and on Inexact fall back to a remainder-distribution algorithm.

Read getcontext().flags after an untrapped operation to detect that rounding happened, and use ctx.clear_flags() to get a clean reading; understand that localcontext() isolates flags so the surrounding program is unaffected.
Intentionally raise/catch InvalidOperation to validate user-supplied money strings, translating it into a domain-meaningful ValueError.
Appreciate why production financial code enforces exactness: a payment allocation must sum back to the original total to the cent, with no money invented or lost.
tasks.erp_increment: Adds penny-exact money helpers to the MiniERP money module that Sales & Invoicing and Payments depend on. (1) parse_money(text) parses a user/import-supplied amount by trapping nothing extra but catching the default-trapped InvalidOperation and re-raising it as a clear ValueError('not valid money: ...') — this is the first place the ERP deliberately exercises InvalidOperation, the signal later phases only reference. (2) split_payment(total, parts) splits an invoice total across N installments/line items: it first attempts an exact equal share inside localcontext() with traps[Inexact] = True, and only if that raises Inexact does it fall back to a largest-remainder allocation (floor each share with ROUND_DOWN, then hand out the leftover cents one at a time) so the shares always sum back to the original total exactly. (3) had_rounding(total, parts) reports, via getcontext().flags[Inexact] after a cleared-flag division, whether an even split would have lost precision — letting reporting flag invoices that don't divide cleanly. These build only on the Decimal money quantization/rounding work from the earlier decimal-money task; no classes or custom exception types are required yet.
tasks.starter_stub:
from decimal import (
    Decimal, localcontext, getcontext,
    Inexact, InvalidOperation, ROUND_DOWN,
)

CENTS = Decimal("0.01")  # 2-decimal money quantum, as in the earlier decimal-money stage


def parse_money(text: str) -> Decimal:
    """Parse a money string to a cent-quantized Decimal.

    Decimal('not-a-number') raises InvalidOperation (it is trapped by default in
    the standard context). Catch it and re-raise a domain ValueError so callers
    in Sales & Invoicing get a clear message instead of a raw decimal signal.
    """
    try:
        return Decimal(text).quantize(CENTS)
    except InvalidOperation:
        raise ValueError(f"not valid money: {text!r}")


def split_payment(total: Decimal, parts: int) -> list[Decimal]:
    """Split `total` across `parts` shares that sum back to `total` exactly.

    Step 1 — try an exact equal split. Inside localcontext() set
    ``ctx.traps[Inexact] = True`` so that ``(total / parts).quantize(CENTS)``
    RAISES Inexact when the division cannot be represented exactly in cents.
    If it does not raise, return ``[share] * parts``.

    Step 2 — on Inexact, fall back to largest-remainder allocation:
    floor each share with ``.quantize(CENTS, rounding=ROUND_DOWN)``, then
    distribute the leftover cents (total - floored_sum) one cent at a time to
    the first shares. The returned list MUST sum to `total` exactly.
    """
    # TODO: implement Step 1 (trap Inexact) and Step 2 (remainder distribution).
    raise NotImplementedError


def had_rounding(total: Decimal, parts: int) -> bool:
    """Return True if an even split of `total` across `parts` would lose precision.

    Do NOT trap anything. Inside localcontext(): clear_flags(), perform the
    division (no quantize), then read ``ctx.flags[Inexact]``. Because the
    context is local, the surrounding program's flags are untouched.
    """
    # TODO: clear flags, divide, then return the Inexact flag's value.
    raise NotImplementedError


if __name__ == "__main__":
    total = parse_money("100.00")
    for n in (4, 3):
        shares = split_payment(total, n)
        print(f"{total} / {n} -> {[str(s) for s in shares]}  {sum(shares)=}  {had_rounding(total, n)=}")
tasks.test_focus: Verify the trap/flag machinery end to end. (1) parse_money: valid strings quantize to 2dp ('19.5' -> Decimal('19.50')); invalid strings ('twelve', '') raise ValueError (not a bare InvalidOperation leaking out). (2) split_payment: an exact case ('100.00', 4) returns four equal Decimal('25.00') shares; an inexact case ('100.00', 3) returns ['33.34','33.33','33.33']; assert across several totals/parts (incl. '9.99'/2, '0.10'/3) that sum(shares) == total EXACTLY and len(shares) == parts and every share is cent-quantized. (3) had_rounding: True for ('100.00', 3) and False for ('100.00', 4). (4) Isolation check: call getcontext().clear_flags(), invoke had_rounding/split_payment, and assert getcontext().flags[Inexact] is still False afterward — proving localcontext() did not leak signal flags into the global context. Optionally assert that an exact split_payment did not need the remainder fallback by checking the shares are all identical.

tasks.title: Custom codec error handlers: lossless export to legacy systems
python_features: custom error-handler callables (signature taking a UnicodeError, returning a (replacement, resume_index) tuple)
str.encode(encoding, errors) and bytes.decode(encoding, errors) with the errors= argument
surrogateescape round-tripping of undecodable bytes (\udc80-\udcff smuggling)
slicing a UnicodeError's .object to read the offending code unit(s)
learning_goals: Explain what an error handler is in Python's codec machinery and when each of the six built-in handlers ('strict', 'replace', 'ignore', 'backslashreplace', 'xmlcharrefreplace', 'namereplace', plus 'surrogateescape' for decoding) is the right tool.
Read the attributes of a UnicodeEncodeError / UnicodeDecodeError (.object, .start, .end, .reason, .encoding) to inspect exactly which characters could not be represented.

Write a custom error handler: a callable that receives a UnicodeError and returns a (replacement_str_or_bytes, resume_index) tuple, then register it under a namespaced name with codecs.register_error so it can be passed as errors='...' to any encode/decode call.
Use surrogateescape to losslessly round-trip bytes that are not valid in the declared encoding, so a messy imported CSV can be re-exported byte-for-byte.

Choose the correct handler for a downstream consumer: backslashreplace/namereplace for debuggable logs, xmlcharrefreplace for an XML/HTML export feed, a custom transliterating handler for an ASCII-only accounting partner.
tasks.erp_increment: Extends the Import/Export module's text layer so MiniERP can hand its catalog to systems that don't speak UTF-8 without ever crashing on an exotic character and without silently corrupting data. You implement an export_record(record, encoding, errors) helper that encodes a customer/product row (names with accents, currency symbols, the em dash in notes) to a target byte encoding using a caller-chosen handler, and a custom handler registered via codecs.register_error('minierp.xref', ...) that replaces any unencodable character with a stable, reversible '[U+XXXX NAME]' token (built from the UnicodeEncodeError attributes and unicodedata.name) so an ASCII-only partner feed stays human-auditable. You also add import_bytes(raw, encoding) that decodes a partner's CSV with errors='surrogateescape' so undecodable bytes survive a later re-export byte-for-byte. This makes the export feed (built in p02 as plain UTF-8 bytes) robust for the legacy/EDI integrations the Reporting and Import/Export modules need later.
tasks.starter_stub: In task.py provide a customer/product record (a dict with fields like name='Renée Müller', note='priority — VIP', price_label='49,99 €'), an unfinished export_record(record, encoding='ascii', errors='strict') that joins fields and calls str.encode, a stub xref_handler(err) the learner completes to return ('[U+%04X %s]' % (cp, unicodedata.name(chr(cp), '?')), err.start+1), a codecs.register_error('minierp.xref', xref_handler) registration line, and import_bytes(raw, encoding) using decode(..., 'surrogateescape'). Seed TODOs where the learner fills the handler body and wires errors= through.
tasks.test_focus: Verify: (1) export_record(..., errors='strict') raises UnicodeEncodeError on a non-ASCII record while errors='replace'/'ignore' do not; (2) the four named handlers produce their documented bytes (e.g. backslashreplace yields b'\\u2014' for the em dash, xmlcharrefreplace yields b'—', namereplace yields b'\\N{EM DASH}'); (3) codecs.lookup_error('minierp.xref') returns the registered handler and export_record(..., errors='minierp.xref') turns 'é' into b'[U+00E9 LATIN SMALL LETTER E WITH ACUTE]' with surrounding ASCII intact; (4) the custom handler advances correctly over consecutive non-ASCII chars (no infinite loop, no skipped characters); (5) import_bytes(b'caf\xe9', 'utf-8') decodes via surrogateescape and re-encoding with 'surrogateescape' reproduces the original bytes exactly; (6) a UnicodeEncodeError caught in the test exposes .start/.end/.object pointing at the offending character.

manifest.phase_title: Built-in Data Structures: The In-Memory Catalog & Inventory
manifest.erp_milestone: MiniERP holds its product catalog and inventory entirely in memory using the right container for each job — products in a dict keyed by SKU, tags in sets, line items as tuples, stock levels in a Counter, multi-location stock via ChainMap — and produces its first reports (low-stock lists, tag rollups, price brackets, inventory valuation) purely with comprehensions, unpacking, and the walrus operator. The result is a query-able catalog module that all four front-ends will later read from.

lessons.title: Lesson 1 — Sequences: Lists, Tuples, Ranges & Slicing
lessons.summary: Build the catalog's backbone out of sequences. Learners represent each product as a tuple record, collect products into a list, model SKU ranges, and learn mutability the hard way (lists alias, tuples don't). Deep slicing powers pagination and 'latest N products'. Unpacking — including starred targets — destructures records cleanly. Every task edits the single evolving catalog module so the code accumulates.

tasks.title: Why containers? Sequences as the catalog's spine

tasks.erp_increment: Introduces the design decision for this phase: the catalog will be an in-memory list of product records (tuples), and explains the roadmap (dicts, sets, Counter come in later lessons). No code change yet — sets the architecture the next tasks implement.

lessons.title: Lesson 2 — Dicts, Sets & Comprehensions: Indexing and Reporting the Catalog

lessons.title: Lesson 3 — The collections Module: Specialized Containers for Inventory
lessons.summary: Upgrade the inventory engine with the right specialized container for each job: namedtuple for self-documenting records, Counter for stock-on-hand and best-sellers, defaultdict for grouping, deque for a bounded recent-activity log, OrderedDict for move_to_end LRU behavior, ChainMap for layered multi-location stock and config, and UserDict/UserList to build a validating catalog wrapper. Also covers PEP 448 spread in calls/literals to tie the structures together.

tasks.title: namedtuple: self-documenting product records

Access fields by name and create modified copies with _replace
Convert to a dict with _asdict for serialization
Confirm namedtuples still unpack and index like plain tuples

    """Return a copy with price_cents replaced (use _replace)."""

tasks.title: __missing__: how defaultdict really works (auto-zeroing the stock ledger)
python_features: d[key] subscription vs dict.get() vs the in / __contains__ operator (which bypass __missing__)
auto-populating a key from inside __missing__ via self[key] = ...
KeyError and the default dict behaviour __missing__ overrides
self-referential mutating subscript (ledger[sku] += qty) enabled by __missing__
f-string '=' self-documenting debug form (optional, in docstring examples)
learning_goals: Explain that Python's dict calls __missing__(self, key) ONLY when a normal d[key] lookup fails on a dict *subclass* — and that this is the exact hook collections.defaultdict uses under the hood.
Override __missing__ so an absent SKU resolves to a freshly inserted 0 balance, letting `ledger[sku] += qty` work without any `if sku not in ...` guard.
Distinguish the three access paths precisely: d[key] triggers __missing__; dict.get(key) and `key in d` do NOT (they never insert and never call your hook).
Recognise that __missing__ is the idiomatic, allocation-light alternative to pre-seeding a plain dict or pre-checking membership, and reimplement defaultdict's behaviour by hand to demystify it.
Understand that returning a value from __missing__ does not by itself store it — you must explicitly assign self[key] = value to make the key persist (the auto-populate pattern).
tasks.erp_increment: Adds an inventory stock-balance ledger to the Inventory module. A new dict-subclass `StockLedger` maps SKU -> on-hand quantity (int). Its `__missing__(self, sku)` auto-inserts a 0 balance for any SKU seen for the first time, so applying a stream of stock movements becomes `for sku, delta in movements: ledger[sku] += delta` with no membership guards. A module-level `apply_movements(movements)` builds a `StockLedger` from an iterable of (sku, delta) pairs (positive for receipts, negative for sales/shipments) and returns it, giving Reporting a clean running-balance view. This is the hand-rolled version of `defaultdict(int)`; later inventory tasks can swap in or compare against defaultdict knowing exactly what it does. Relies only on plain dict subclassing and integer arithmetic already covered in earlier phases.
tasks.starter_stub:
class StockLedger(dict):
    """A SKU -> on-hand-quantity map whose missing keys default to 0.

    Subscripting an unknown SKU must INSERT a 0 balance and return it, so
    that ``ledger[sku] += delta`` works for first-time SKUs. This is the
    same mechanism ``collections.defaultdict(int)`` uses internally.
    """

    def __missing__(self, sku: str) -> int:
        # TODO: insert a 0 balance for `sku`, then return it.
        # Note: dict.get(sku) and `sku in ledger` must NOT trigger this.
        ...


def apply_movements(movements) -> StockLedger:
    """Fold an iterable of (sku, delta) movements into a StockLedger.

    delta > 0 -> receipt/return; delta < 0 -> sale/shipment.
    Unknown SKUs start at 0 thanks to StockLedger.__missing__.
    """
    ledger = StockLedger()
    # TODO: for sku, delta in movements: ledger[sku] += delta
    ...
    return ledger
tasks.test_focus: Verify __missing__ semantics and the ERP behaviour, not just the happy path. Tests: (1) a fresh StockLedger()[ 'SKU-NEW' ] returns 0 AND inserts the key (assert 'SKU-NEW' in ledger afterward, len grows) — proving auto-populate. (2) ledger['X'] += 3 then += 2 yields 5 starting from empty (no pre-seed). (3) .get('UNSEEN') returns None and .get('UNSEEN', -1) returns -1 WITHOUT inserting the key (len unchanged) — proving .get bypasses __missing__. (4) 'UNSEEN' in ledger is False and does NOT insert — proving __contains__/in bypasses __missing__. (5) apply_movements([('A',10),('B',5),('A',-3),('C',0)]) == {'A':7,'B':5,'C':0}; an empty iterable yields an empty ledger. (6) confirm StockLedger is a dict subclass (isinstance(ledger, dict)) and behaves identically to defaultdict(int) on the same movement stream (compare against collections.defaultdict(int) as an oracle). (7) reading a present key never calls __missing__ (set ledger['P']=9; assert ledger['P']==9 and len unchanged).

manifest.erp_milestone: A command dispatcher built on match/case that routes ERP commands to handler functions, backed by a pure business-rules layer for pricing, tax, and tiered/coupon discounts. By the end of this phase MiniERP can parse a command line into a structured request, dispatch it via structural pattern matching over literal/sequence/mapping/class/OR patterns with guards, and compute a fully-itemized order total (unit price, line discounts, customer-tier discount, coupons, tax, recursive bundle expansion) using functions that exercise default/positional-only/keyword-only/var-args parameters, closures, and higher-order helpers.

lessons.title: Branching, Loops, and the Pricing Engine
lessons.summary: Build the foundation of MiniERP's business-rules layer using conditional logic and iteration. Learners start from the in-memory catalog built in earlier phases and add a pricing engine: applying if/elif/else tiered pricing, ternary expressions for compact defaults, for/while loops to total a cart, and the rare for-else / while-else constructs plus break/continue/pass to express real inventory-search and validation logic. Every task evolves the same pricing module forward.

tasks.title: Tiered Pricing with if/elif/else
learning_goals: Write multi-branch decisions with if/elif/else and understand that only the first matching branch runs
Use chained comparisons to express quantity bands cleanly
Reason about truthiness and short-circuiting in boolean conditions
tasks.erp_increment: Create pricing.py with quantity_tier(qty) returning a discount-tier label ('none'/'bulk'/'wholesale') from quantity bands, and unit_price_for(base_price, qty) that selects a per-unit price using if/elif/else over those bands. This is the first stone of the business-rules layer that later tasks extend.

    ...


def unit_price_for(base_price: float, qty: int) -> float:
    """Per-unit price after the quantity-tier multiplier."""
    # none x1.0, bulk x0.95, wholesale x0.90
    ...
tasks.test_focus: Boundary quantities (1, 9, 10, 49, 50, 100) map to correct tiers; unit_price_for applies the right multiplier at each boundary; invalid qty<=0 raises ValueError.

tasks.title: Conditional Expressions for Compact Defaults
python_features: conditional expression (a if cond else b)
learning_goals: Use the ternary conditional expression where a value, not a branch, is needed
Recognize when a ternary improves readability and when a full if-statement is clearer
Distinguish expressions (produce a value) from statements (perform an action)
tasks.erp_increment: Add display_price(price, currency='USD') to pricing.py that formats a price with a ternary picking the symbol ('$' if USD else the ISO code), and round_money(x) that uses a ternary to clamp tiny negative rounding artifacts to 0.0. These helpers are reused by every later total calculation.
tasks.test_focus: display_price returns symbol form for USD and code form otherwise; round_money clamps near-zero negatives to 0.0 and rounds to 2 decimals.

tasks.title: Totalling a Cart with for
python_features: tuple unpacking in the loop target
learning_goals: Iterate a sequence and accumulate a running total
Unpack each line item directly in the for target
Reuse earlier pricing helpers inside a loop
tasks.erp_increment: Add cart_subtotal(lines) to pricing.py where lines is a list of (sku, base_price, qty) tuples; loop over them, call unit_price_for, and accumulate the rounded subtotal. This is MiniERP's first end-to-end order calculation.
tasks.test_focus: Empty cart returns 0.0; single and multi-line carts total correctly with tier multipliers applied; result is rounded to 2 decimals.

tasks.title: Reservation Loop with while
learning_goals: Choose while when the iteration count is condition-driven, not sequence-driven
Decrement a counter safely and terminate with break
Recognize and avoid infinite-loop pitfalls
tasks.erp_increment: Add reserve_units(available, requested) to an inventory.py module: using a while loop, draw down stock one batch at a time (batch size 10) until the request is met or stock runs out, returning units actually reserved. Models partial fulfillment for later Sales tasks.
tasks.test_focus: Reserves exactly requested when stock suffices; caps at available when short; handles requested=0 and available=0 without looping forever.

tasks.title: Catalog Search with for/else
learning_goals: Use the rare for/else to express 'searched everything and found nothing'
Understand that else runs only when the loop completes without break
Combine continue to skip non-matching items
tasks.erp_increment: Add find_in_catalog(catalog, sku) to inventory.py that linearly scans the in-memory catalog (built in earlier phases), continues past mismatches, breaks on a hit returning the product, and uses the for/else to raise KeyError('unknown sku') when the loop exhausts without breaking.
tasks.test_focus: Returns the matching product dict; raises KeyError for an absent sku (proves the else branch fired); skips earlier non-matches via continue.

tasks.title: Batch Validation with while/else
learning_goals: Use the rare while/else to signal 'all items validated, none rejected'
Use break to short-circuit on the first invalid item
Use pass as an explicit do-nothing placeholder branch
tasks.erp_increment: Add all_lines_valid(lines) to pricing.py: walk the cart with an index-driven while loop, break on the first line with qty<=0 or base_price<0, and use the while/else to return True only when every line passed. A pass branch documents the 'valid line, nothing to do' case.
tasks.test_focus: Returns True for all-valid carts (else fired); returns False on first invalid line (break taken); empty cart returns True.

tasks.title: break, continue, pass, and the else clauses
python_features: break vs continue vs pass semantics
learning_goals: Predict exactly when a loop's else clause executes
Distinguish break (exit), continue (skip), and pass (no-op)
Avoid the common misconception that else runs 'at the end always'
tasks.erp_increment: No code change: a knowledge check consolidating the loop-control semantics used by find_in_catalog and all_lines_valid before the dispatcher work begins, ensuring learners can reason about the control flow they just wrote.
tasks.starter_stub:
# Multiple-choice knowledge check; no code to edit.
# Q: In `for x in xs: ... else: ...`, when does the else block run?
#   A) Always after the loop body
#   B) Only if the loop completes without hitting break  <-- correct
#   C) Only if xs is empty
#   D) Never, else is illegal after for
tasks.test_focus: Single correct option: the else clause runs only when the loop finishes without executing break.

lessons.title: Structural Pattern Matching & the Command Dispatcher
lessons.summary: Deliver the phase's headline ERP feature: a command dispatcher driven entirely by match/case. Learners progress through every flavor of structural pattern matching — literal and capture patterns, the wildcard, sequence patterns with star-capture, mapping patterns, class patterns using __match_args__, OR patterns, guards, and nested patterns — each task expanding the same dispatch() function so MiniERP can route real commands (add, price, discount, report) parsed from a CLI-style token list into structured request objects.

tasks.title: Literal and Capture Patterns
learning_goals: Write a basic match with literal cases and a catch-all wildcard
Distinguish a literal pattern from a capture pattern that binds a variable
Model a closed command vocabulary with StrEnum
tasks.erp_increment: Create cli/dispatch.py with a Command(StrEnum) of verbs (ADD, PRICE, DISCOUNT, REPORT, HELP) and normalize_verb(token) that matches a raw string literal to the canonical Command via match/case, capturing unknown tokens into a default and returning Command.HELP for anything unrecognized.
tasks.starter_stub:
from enum import StrEnum


class Command(StrEnum):
    ADD = "add"
    PRICE = "price"
    DISCOUNT = "discount"
    REPORT = "report"
    HELP = "help"


def normalize_verb(token: str) -> Command:
    """Map a raw token to a Command via match/case; unknown -> HELP."""
    match token.strip().lower():
        case "add":
            return Command.ADD
        case _:
            ...
tasks.test_focus: Known verbs map to the right Command members including case/whitespace normalization; unknown tokens fall through the wildcard to Command.HELP.

tasks.title: Sequence Patterns and the Token Parser
learning_goals: Destructure an argv-style token list with sequence patterns
Capture a variable-length tail using *rest
Match on structure (arity) rather than writing manual length checks
tasks.erp_increment: Add parse_tokens(tokens) to cli/dispatch.py that matches the token list: [] -> HELP request, [verb] -> bare command, [verb, *args] -> command with captured args. Returns a (Command, args) pair, giving the dispatcher its structured input from a CLI line.
tasks.starter_stub:
def parse_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> tuple[Command, list[str]]:
    """Destructure argv-style tokens with sequence patterns."""
    match tokens:
        case []:
            return (Command.HELP, [])
        case [verb]:
            ...
        case [verb, *args]:
            ...
tasks.test_focus: Empty list yields a HELP request; single token yields verb with empty args; multi-token yields verb plus the exact captured tail list.

tasks.title: Mapping Patterns for Structured Requests
learning_goals: Match dict-shaped requests by required keys
Capture both a known field and the rest of a mapping
Understand that mapping patterns match on subset, not exact, keys
tasks.erp_increment: Add classify_request(req) to cli/dispatch.py that takes a dict request (e.g. from a Web/JSON interface) and matches mapping patterns like {'op': 'price', 'sku': sku, **rest} to extract the operation and salient fields, routing JSON-style payloads through the same logic the CLI uses.
tasks.starter_stub:
def classify_request(req: dict) -> tuple[Command, dict]:
    """Match a dict payload with mapping patterns; capture remaining keys with **rest."""
    match req:
        case {"op": "price", "sku": sku, **rest}:
            ...
        case {"op": op}:
            ...
        case _:
            return (Command.HELP, {})
tasks.test_focus: A price payload extracts sku and leftover fields via **rest; a generic {'op':...} payload routes by op; a payload missing 'op' falls to HELP.

tasks.title: Class Patterns with __match_args__
learning_goals: Match against object types and destructure their fields positionally and by keyword
Define __match_args__ (directly or via dataclass) to enable positional class patterns
Dispatch on typed request objects rather than raw dicts
tasks.erp_increment: Add request dataclasses (AddRequest, PriceRequest, DiscountRequest) with slots/kw_only to cli/dispatch.py and a describe(req) that uses class patterns — PriceRequest(sku=s, qty=q), AddRequest(sku, qty) — to render a human summary, formalizing the typed command objects the dispatcher will execute.
tasks.starter_stub:
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass(slots=True, kw_only=True)
class PriceRequest:
    sku: str
    qty: int


def describe(req: object) -> str:
    """Render a summary using class patterns over request dataclasses."""
    match req:
        case PriceRequest(sku=s, qty=q):
            ...
        case _:
            return "unknown request"
tasks.test_focus: Each request dataclass matches its class pattern and binds the right fields; an unrelated object falls to the wildcard summary.

tasks.title: OR Patterns and Guards
learning_goals: Collapse equivalent cases with the | OR pattern
Refine a case with an if-guard evaluated after the pattern binds
Order cases so the most specific guarded case wins
tasks.erp_increment: Add route_command(cmd, args) to cli/dispatch.py: an alias OR pattern (Command.HELP | Command.REPORT) shares a read-only handler, while a guard (case Command.PRICE if args) requires arguments before pricing and otherwise emits a usage error — encoding real dispatch rules.
tasks.starter_stub:
def route_command(cmd: Command, args: list[str]) -> str:
    """Route with OR patterns and guards."""
    match cmd:
        case Command.HELP | Command.REPORT:
            return "read-only"
        case Command.PRICE if args:
            ...
        case Command.PRICE:
            return "usage: price <sku> <qty>"
        case _:
            ...
tasks.test_focus: HELP and REPORT share the read-only branch; PRICE with args routes to pricing; PRICE without args hits the usage guard; unknown verbs fall through.

tasks.title: Nested Patterns and the Full Dispatcher
python_features: match as the single dispatch entry point
learning_goals: Compose sequence, mapping, and class patterns to one level deep and beyond
Design a single match that handles the whole command surface
See how structural matching replaces sprawling if/elif chains
tasks.erp_increment: Add dispatch(request) to cli/dispatch.py — the phase's milestone entry point — matching nested shapes like ['add', sku, qty] and {'op': 'discount', 'rules': [*rules]} and PriceRequest(...) in one match, producing the typed request object that the pricing layer in Lesson 3 will execute. Wire main.py to call dispatch() so MiniERP has a working command front door.
tasks.starter_stub:
def dispatch(request) -> object:
    """Single match/case entry point over CLI lists, dict payloads, and request objects."""
    match request:
        case ["add", sku, qty]:
            ...
        case {"op": "discount", "rules": [*rules]}:
            ...
        case PriceRequest() as req:
            return req
        case _:
            ...
tasks.test_focus: List, dict, and object forms each route to the correct typed request; nested rules list is captured; malformed input yields a HELP/error sentinel.

lessons.title: Functions, Closures, and the Discount Rules Layer
lessons.summary: Complete the business-rules layer by mastering Python's function model. Learners build composable discount and tax functions exercising every parameter kind (defaults and the mutable-default gotcha, *args, keyword-only, positional-only, **kwargs), annotations and docstrings, lambdas and higher-order helpers (map/filter/sorted/min/max/any/all/zip/enumerate with sort keys), closures with the late-binding gotcha, LEGB scoping with nonlocal/global, and recursion with the recursion limit. The result is a pricing engine that computes a fully itemized order total and a dispatcher wired to executable handlers.

tasks.title: Default Arguments and the Mutable-Default Gotcha
python_features: the shared mutable-default gotcha (def f(x, acc=[]))
learning_goals: Define functions with sensible default arguments
Diagnose and fix the mutable-default-argument bug using the None sentinel
Document behavior with a docstring and annotate parameters
tasks.erp_increment: Add apply_percent(price, percent=0.0) and accumulate_discounts(amount, applied=None) to rules.py: the accumulator deliberately uses the None-sentinel idiom so the discount-history list is not shared across calls — fixing a class of bug that would corrupt MiniERP totals.
tasks.test_focus: apply_percent with default returns price unchanged and applies percents correctly; repeated accumulate_discounts calls do NOT share state (proves the gotcha is avoided).

tasks.title: Stacking Discounts with *args and **kwargs
learning_goals: Accept an arbitrary number of positional discount percents with *args
Accept open-ended named options with **kwargs
Unpack collections into a call with * and **
tasks.erp_increment: Add stack_discounts(price, *percents, **options) to rules.py that sequentially applies each percent in *percents and reads options like clamp_floor from **kwargs, letting Sales stack promotional discounts of unknown count onto a line price.
tasks.starter_stub:
def stack_discounts(price: float, *percents: float, **options) -> float:
    """Apply each percent in turn; honor options like clamp_floor=<min price>."""
    result = price
    for p in percents:
        ...
    floor = options.get("clamp_floor", 0.0)
    ...
tasks.test_focus: Zero, one, and many percents stack multiplicatively; clamp_floor option prevents the price dropping below the floor; unpacking *list/**dict at the call site works.

tasks.title: Positional-Only and Keyword-Only Parameters
learning_goals: Force callers to pass certain args by keyword using the * separator
Lock certain args as positional-only using the / separator
Design signatures that read clearly and resist breaking changes
tasks.erp_increment: Add apply_tax(amount, /, *, rate, inclusive=False) to rules.py: amount is positional-only (an internal detail) while rate and inclusive are keyword-only (must be explicit at the call site), making MiniERP's tax calls self-documenting and refactor-safe.
tasks.starter_stub:
def apply_tax(amount: float, /, *, rate: float, inclusive: bool = False) -> float:
    """amount is positional-only; rate and inclusive are keyword-only.
    inclusive=False -> add tax on top; True -> back tax out of amount."""
    ...
tasks.test_focus: Exclusive tax adds rate*amount; inclusive tax extracts embedded tax; passing rate positionally raises TypeError; passing amount by keyword raises TypeError.

tasks.title: Lambdas, Sort Keys, and Higher-Order Builtins
learning_goals: Write small anonymous functions with lambda for keys and predicates
Sort and select cart lines by computed keys
Use map/filter/any/all/enumerate/zip to express bulk operations declaratively
tasks.erp_increment: Add reporting helpers to rules.py: rank_lines(lines) sorts cart lines by line total via sorted(key=lambda ...), cheapest_line/dearest_line use min/max with keys, line_numbers uses enumerate, and any_over_limit uses any() — the analytics primitives the Reporting module will surface.
tasks.test_focus: rank_lines orders by descending line total; min/max pick the right extremes; any_over_limit is True only when some line exceeds the limit; enumerate numbering is 1-based as specified.

tasks.title: Closures and the Late-Binding Gotcha
python_features: the late-binding closure gotcha in loops
learning_goals: Create configured functions with a closure factory
Explain and fix the late-binding bug where loop closures share the final value
Treat functions as first-class values stored in lists/dicts
tasks.erp_increment: Add make_coupon(percent) to rules.py returning a closure that discounts a price, and build_coupon_table(percents) that creates one coupon per percent — correctly using the default-arg capture trick to dodge the late-binding gotcha, so MiniERP's coupon registry behaves as intended.
tasks.test_focus: make_coupon closures apply their captured percent; build_coupon_table coupons each apply distinct percents (proves late-binding gotcha avoided), not all the last one.

tasks.title: LEGB Scope, nonlocal, and global
python_features: why reassignment needs a scope declaration
learning_goals: Trace name resolution through Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in scopes
Use nonlocal to mutate an enclosing function's variable
Use global deliberately (and understand why it is usually avoided)
tasks.erp_increment: Add make_running_total() to rules.py returning an add(n) closure that updates an enclosing accumulator via nonlocal, plus a module-level audit counter bumped through global in record_pricing_call() — instrumentation the Audit-log module will later consume.
tasks.starter_stub:
_PRICING_CALLS = 0


def record_pricing_call() -> int:
    """Increment the module-level counter using global; return new count."""
    global _PRICING_CALLS
    ...


def make_running_total():
    total = 0.0
    def add(n: float) -> float:
        nonlocal total
        ...
    return add
tasks.test_focus: Successive add() calls accumulate via nonlocal; record_pricing_call increments the module global across calls; omitting nonlocal/global would rebind locally (covered by the contrast in tests).

tasks.title: Recursion and the Recursion Limit
python_features: base case and recursive case design
learning_goals: Write a recursive function with a correct base case
Reason about stack depth and the interpreter recursion limit
Decide when recursion is clearer than a loop and when it risks RecursionError
tasks.erp_increment: Add expand_bundle(catalog, sku, qty) to rules.py that recursively explodes a product bundle (a product whose components are themselves products, possibly bundles) into flat (sku, qty) leaf lines, with cycle/depth protection referencing sys.getrecursionlimit — the final pricing primitive enabling MiniERP to price composite kit products.
tasks.starter_stub:
import sys


def expand_bundle(catalog: list[dict], sku: str, qty: int = 1, _depth: int = 0) -> list[tuple[str, int]]:
    """Recursively flatten a bundle into leaf (sku, qty) lines.
    Base case: a non-bundle product. Guard against runaway depth."""
    if _depth > 50:
        raise RecursionError("bundle nesting too deep")
    ...
tasks.test_focus: Flat product returns one line; one- and multi-level bundles flatten with multiplied quantities; over-deep/cyclic nesting raises RecursionError via the depth guard.

tasks.title: Assemble the Order-Total Pipeline
python_features: function annotations for the public API
learning_goals: Compose many small functions into one clear pipeline
Expose a well-annotated, well-documented public entry point
Connect the dispatcher to executable pricing handlers
tasks.erp_increment: Add order_total(catalog, lines, *, tier_percent=0.0, coupons=(), tax_rate=0.0) to rules.py that expands bundles, computes the subtotal, stacks tier and coupon discounts, applies keyword-only tax, and returns an itemized breakdown dict. Wire dispatch()'s PRICE/DISCOUNT routes to call it, completing the phase milestone: a match/case dispatcher backed by a full pricing/discount rules layer.
tasks.starter_stub:
def order_total(catalog: list[dict], lines: list[tuple[str, float, int]], *,
               tier_percent: float = 0.0, coupons: tuple = (), tax_rate: float = 0.0) -> dict:
    """Full pipeline: expand bundles -> subtotal -> tier+coupon discounts -> tax.
    Returns an itemized breakdown: {'subtotal':..., 'discount':..., 'tax':..., 'total':...}."""
    ...
tasks.test_focus: Breakdown fields are internally consistent (subtotal - discount + tax == total); tier_percent, coupons, and tax_rate each move the total correctly; passing tax_rate positionally is rejected; dispatch() PRICE route returns this breakdown.

manifest.erp_milestone: Replace the dict-and-function records from earlier phases with real domain classes. Product, Money, Customer, LineItem, and Invoice become proper objects with constructors, validated/computed attributes, human-readable display, value equality, ordering, arithmetic, truthiness/length protocols, and memory-tight __slots__ — forming the shared domain layer that every later interface (CLI, Web, GUI, TUI) will sit on top of.

lessons.title: From Records to Objects
lessons.summary: Learners take the loose dicts and helper functions that represented a product catalog in earlier phases and rebuild them as classes. This lesson establishes the absolute core of Python objects: what a class and an instance are, how self threads state through methods, the difference between __init__ (initializer) and __new__ (constructor/allocator), the sharp distinction between class attributes (shared) and instance attributes (per-object), plain methods, and the two alternative method flavors @classmethod and @staticmethod. By the end the catalog is a real Product class with multiple construction paths.

tasks.title: Why Objects? Dicts vs. Classes
learning_goals: Understand the limits of dict-based records (no validation, typo-prone keys, no behavior co-located with data)
Form a mental model of a class as a blueprint and an instance as one filled-in copy
Understand that self is just the current instance passed as the first argument
Preview how methods bundle behavior next to data
tasks.erp_increment: Frame the refactor: the existing product dicts like {'sku': 'A1', 'name': 'Widget', 'price_cents': 1500} and the free functions that operated on them will become a Product class. No code yet — this page sets the target shape of the domain layer.
tasks.starter_stub:
# Reading task — no code to write.
# Earlier phases stored a product as a dict and passed it to functions:
#
#     product = {"sku": "A1", "name": "Widget", "price_cents": 1500}
#     def product_label(p): return f"{p['sku']} - {p['name']}"
#
# This lesson rebuilds that as a class so data and behavior live together.
tasks.test_focus: No checks (theory page).

tasks.title: Define the Product Class
learning_goals: Write a class with __init__ that stores instance attributes
Understand self as the first parameter of every instance method
Add an instance method that uses self.<attr> to compute a result
Construct instances and read their attributes
tasks.erp_increment: Create the Product domain class with sku, name, and price_cents instance attributes set in __init__, plus a label() method returning 'SKU - Name'. This is the first real domain object in MiniERP.
tasks.test_focus: Construct a Product, assert each instance attribute is stored correctly, and assert label() returns the 'SKU - Name' format.

tasks.title: Shared vs. Per-Object State
learning_goals: Distinguish a class attribute (shared by all instances) from an instance attribute (unique per object)
See how assigning to self.x shadows a class attribute x for that instance
Recognize the classic mutable-class-attribute bug and why instance state belongs in __init__
Use a class attribute for a true constant shared across the catalog
tasks.erp_increment: Add a class attribute CURRENCY = 'USD' shared by every Product, and a class-attribute count or DEFAULT_TAX_RATE constant, while keeping sku/name/price per-instance — clarifying which catalog facts are global vs. per-product.
tasks.test_focus: Assert Product.CURRENCY is readable on both the class and an instance; assert two distinct instances share the class attribute but hold independent sku/name/price; demonstrate that setting price on one instance does not change another.

tasks.title: Behavior Lives on the Object
python_features: methods calling other methods via self
learning_goals: Move former free functions onto the class as methods
Call one method from another through self
Understand that a method is a function whose first argument is the instance
Co-locate behavior with the data it operates on
tasks.erp_increment: Migrate the old free functions (e.g. product_label, price_display) into Product methods such as label() and price_display() returning '$15.00', so the catalog's behavior now lives on the object itself.
tasks.test_focus: Assert price_display() formats cents as '$15.00'; assert summary() composes label() and price_display() by calling them through self.

tasks.title: Alternate Constructors with @classmethod
learning_goals: Write a @classmethod that receives cls and builds an instance
Understand cls vs self and why factories use cls (subclass-friendly)
Provide multiple named construction paths for one class
Use a class attribute mutated through cls to track instances
tasks.erp_increment: Add Product.from_row(cls, row) to build a Product from a CSV-style tuple/dict (mirroring the import format from earlier phases) and from_dict(cls, data), giving the catalog clean factory entry points for later Import/Export.
tasks.test_focus: Assert from_row builds an equivalent Product to the direct constructor; assert from_dict reads the right keys; assert the factory returns an instance of the class.

tasks.title: Stateless Helpers with @staticmethod
python_features: namespacing utility functions on a class
when static vs class vs instance method
learning_goals: Write a @staticmethod that takes neither self nor cls
Decide when a helper belongs as static (no instance/class state needed)
Group a pure utility under the class as a namespace
Contrast static, class, and instance methods in one place
tasks.erp_increment: Add Product.is_valid_sku(value) as a @staticmethod validating SKU format (e.g. non-empty, alphanumeric) used by the factories and later by import validation — a pure rule that needs no instance.
tasks.test_focus: Assert is_valid_sku accepts valid SKUs and rejects empty/invalid ones; assert it is callable on both the class and an instance without an instance argument.

tasks.title: __new__ vs __init__: Who Builds the Object
learning_goals: Understand that __new__ creates the instance and __init__ initializes it
Override __new__ correctly (return super().__new__(cls)) and see ordering
Learn a legitimate use of __new__: caching/interning instances by key
Know when NOT to override __new__ (most classes never need it)
tasks.erp_increment: Give Product a lightweight SKU-interning cache via __new__ so identical SKUs return the same canonical Product instance (a real catalog dedup concern), while __init__ still sets the fields — demonstrating the two-phase creation explicitly.
tasks.test_focus: Assert constructing two Products with the same SKU returns the identical object (is); assert __new__ runs before __init__; assert a different SKU yields a different object.

lessons.title: Properties and Presentation
lessons.summary: Now that products are objects, this lesson makes them safe and presentable. Learners replace raw public attributes with managed @property getters/setters/deleters and computed (derived) attributes, so prices stay valid and totals are always fresh. They then make objects render well for both developers and end users by implementing __repr__, __str__, and __format__, including custom format specs used by the upcoming reporting layer. A Money value object is introduced as a clean home for currency arithmetic and formatting.

tasks.title: Computed Attributes with @property
learning_goals: Turn a method-like calculation into an attribute accessed without parentheses
Create a read-only computed attribute derived from other state
Understand a property is a descriptor that runs code on attribute access
Keep derived values always consistent instead of stored and stale
tasks.erp_increment: Add a computed price_dollars @property to Product (derived from price_cents) so callers read product.price_dollars; this becomes the canonical display value used across interfaces.
tasks.test_focus: Assert price_dollars is accessed without parentheses and returns price_cents/100; assert it tracks changes to price_cents; assert assigning to the read-only property raises AttributeError.

tasks.title: Validation with a Setter
learning_goals: Pair a getter with a @name.setter to intercept assignment
Store state in a backing attribute (_price_cents) behind the property
Validate on set (reject negative prices) and raise ValueError
Preserve a natural attribute-assignment API while enforcing rules
tasks.erp_increment: Make Product.price_cents a validated property backed by _price_cents that rejects negative values, ensuring no invalid price ever enters the catalog regardless of which interface sets it.
tasks.test_focus: Assert setting a valid price updates the backing field; assert setting a negative price raises ValueError; assert __init__ routes through the setter.

tasks.title: The Deleter and Optional Fields
learning_goals: Implement the third property hook, the deleter, triggered by del obj.attr
Model an optional field (e.g. a discount) that can be cleared
See the complete getter/setter/deleter triad on one attribute
Understand when a deleter is appropriate (resetting/clearing optional state)
tasks.erp_increment: Add an optional discount_cents property to Product with a deleter that clears it back to None, giving the pricing model a clearable per-product discount used later by Sales.
tasks.test_focus: Assert the getter/setter round-trip works; assert `del product.discount_cents` resets the value to None via the deleter; assert an invalid discount raises.

tasks.title: A Money Value Object
learning_goals: Design a small value object that wraps a single concept (money in cents)
Combine __init__, a property, and a classmethod factory learned so far
Avoid float money bugs by storing integer cents
Prepare a reusable currency type for prices, totals, and payments
